title: Investigations on the viscoelastic performance of pressure sensitive adhesives in drug-in-adhesive type transdermal films.
notes: The selected pages (8–10, 14–15) of this paper focus on viscoelastic/rheological characterisation of drug-in-adhesive pressure-sensitive adhesive (PSA) films (elastic modulus G′, creep compliance, Chu's criteria, Flory equation drug solubility estimates). No in vitro permeation or release experiment is described or tabulated in these pages. The tables present (Table IV) give drug solubility estimates from rheological and microscopic data, not permeation/flux/cumulative amount endpoints. Ibuprofen is mentioned only as one of four model drugs in a rheological/viscoelastic context. There are no composition tables with excipient concentrations, no Franz-cell or permeation data, and no endpoint values (amount permeated, flux, Jss) relevant to in vitro permeation or release. Recommended action: manual review of other pages (or supplementary material) for any permeation/release data.

title: Soluplus<sup>®</sup> polymeric nanomicelles improve solubility of BCS-class II drugs.

formulations.evidence_snippet: while the neat drug suspension showed a plateau in the dissolution profile after 4 h (at 53% of released drug)
formulations.notes: Plateau reached at 4 h (53% released), no further increase up to 24 h. Values extracted from text description of Fig. 8.

components.remark: Polyvinyl caprolactam-polyvinyl acetate-polyethylene glycol graft copolymer; 1.5 mM micellar suspension; prepared by direct dissolution method

formulations.evidence_snippet: the SNM-IBU formulation ensured a linear progressive release of the drug, reaching a 74% value after 24 h
formulations.notes: SNM-IBU1 prepared by direct dissolution method. Linear progressive release reaching 74% at 24 h. Values extracted from text description of Fig. 8. Release kinetics follow zero-order model (R²=0.9696).

components.remark: Polyvinyl caprolactam-polyvinyl acetate-polyethylene glycol graft copolymer; prepared by direct dissolution method

formulations.evidence_snippet: Model SNM-IBU1 ... Zero order 0.9696 First order 0.9660 Higuchi 0.8914 Hixson–Crowell 0.9683 Weibull 0.0684 b = 0.2302 Korsmeyer–Peppas 0.8220 n = 0.4760
formulations.notes: Table 8: Release kinetics model fitting. Zero-order R²=0.9696, First-order R²=0.9660, Higuchi R²=0.8914, Hixson-Crowell R²=0.9683, Weibull R²=0.0684 (b=0.2302), Korsmeyer-Peppas R²=0.8220 (n=0.4760). Endpoint value given as best-fit model R².

notes: The paper describes in vitro dissolution/release studies for ibuprofen (IBU) Soluplus® nanomicelles. No formal composition table for the IBU formulation was provided in the selected pages; composition details were extracted from the methods text (page 4): 50 mg IBU dissolved in acetone with 1.725 g Soluplus®, rehydrated to 10 mL → 0.5% w/v IBU and 1.5 mM Soluplus® micellar suspension. Two preparation methods were used (direct dissolution = SNM-IBU1; solvent evaporation/thin film hydration = SNM-IBU2), but only SNM-IBU1 release data were described textually in the selected pages. Release profiles are shown in Fig. 8 (image not provided). Key endpoint values extracted from narrative text: neat IBU suspension plateau at 53% at 4 h; SNM-IBU1 reached 74% at 24 h. Table 8 (release kinetics) is a model-fitting R² table, not a direct endpoint table, but is included as it explicitly tabulates the kinetic parameters for SNM-IBU1. No permeation (flux/Jss) data found. No membrane permeation experiment described — this is a dissolution/release study using dialysis or similar under sink conditions with 70:30 water-methanol receptor medium. Table 7 (lyophilization size data) is not a formulation composition or endpoint table and was excluded. Recommended manual review to retrieve the actual Fig. 8 data points from the figure image.

Endpoint numerical value not explicitly tabulated in the provided pages. notes: No formal composition or endpoint summary table was present in the provided pages (pages 5, 8–11). The article describes a novel 3D-printed diffusion cell design and UV imaging methodology. Formulation details (5% w/w ibuprofen gel, silicone membrane, phosphate buffer receptor medium) and experimental conditions were extracted from narrative text only. Quantitative endpoint values (cumulative permeated amount vs. time) are referenced but not tabulated in the provided pages — they likely appear in figures (e.g., Figure referenced in results) rather than tables. Manual review recommended to check remaining pages for any endpoint or composition tables.

notes: Table 1 (page 11) is a composition/excipient function table listing IMS, PEG 300, and PG with their proposed functions. It is not a numerical endpoint table. The four formulations studied are: IBUGEL 5% w/w gel (IMS + PG), IBULEVE Speed Relief 5% Spray (IMS, no PG), 5% w/w IPA/PEG 300 solution, and 5% w/w IPA/PG solution. Endpoint values in the extracted pages are reported as ranges in the abstract/results text (not in a dedicated numerical results table): PAMPA+silicone 6 h 91–136 µg/cm²; porcine skin 48 h 59–81 µg/cm²; human skin 48 h 11–38 µg/cm². Dose variation in PAMPA: 1 µL (finite, 90–100 µg/cm² at 6 h, 64–69%), 3 µL (finite, ~280 µg/cm² at 6 h, 60–70%), 30 µL (infinite). Figures 1–3 contain the complete per-formulation numerical data but were not provided as extracted text. Receptor medium inferred as PBS pH 7.4 from materials section. Silicone membrane 250 µm from Samco. PAMPA membrane = synthetic ceramides, cholesterol and free fatty acids. All studies finite dose except 30 µL PAMPA which is described as infinite dose. Individual per-formulation endpoint values within each range cannot be unambiguously assigned from text alone; bounds are attributed to gel (upper) and spray (lower) based on contextual statements. Manual review recommended to confirm figure-derived values.

title: Controlling Supramolecular Structures of Drugs by Light.

formulations.evidence_snippet: The trans-isomer had a lag time of about 18 hours and reached equilibrium after 72 hours (Figure 4C).

notes: No formal composition or endpoint tables were found in the provided pages. The paper describes in vitro permeation through Strat-M® synthetic skin membranes (Figure 4C) for three Ibuprofen formulations — P+-Ibuprofen cis (UV light), P+-Ibuprofen trans (dark), and Ibuprofen sodium — but all endpoint data are reported only in a figure (Figure 4C) without tabulated numeric values in the available text. The API concentration of 8 mM is explicitly stated for the ion-pair permeation experiments. No numeric cumulative permeation or flux values are available from the text. TABLE_TRUNCATED=True; additional pages may contain a supporting information table with numeric permeation data.

Full release curve is shown in Fig. 4d but tabulated values are not provided in the extracted text.

notes: No structured composition or endpoint table was found in the extracted pages. The PDF text cap was reached and the table pages (if any) may not have been included. Formulation details and the single endpoint value (~20% ibuprofen released at 200 min, DPBS, 100-microneedle patch, 1:1 resin:drug ratio) were extracted from narrative text on page 6 and the figure caption for Fig. 4d. Only ibuprofen rows are extracted here. Three endpoint parameters are reported per group: Jmax (μg/cm²/h), Papp (×10⁻⁶ cm/s), and Recovery (%). Formulations are 80% saturated solutions applied to Franz diffusion cells with bovine split skin (600 μm) and PBS receptor fluid. Endpoint times are not explicitly tabulated (Jmax is derived from the linear portion of the permeation curve). Diffusion area ~1.77 cm², receptor volume ~12 mL, n=5–6. Table is truncated; no additional ibuprofen-specific composition table was identified in the provided pages.

title: Effects of Temperature and Humidity on the Skin Permeation of Hydrophilic and Hydrophobic Drugs.

formulations.evidence_snippet: Table III. Skin Permeation Parameters of IP and ANP ... IP T48RH95 −6.04 ± 0.048 2.22 ± 0.20 41.4 ± 7.28
formulations.notes: Environmental condition: T48RH95 (48°C, 95% RH, AH 71.7 g/m³, skin surface temp 40°C). Log P = −6.04 ± 0.048; DL⁻² = 2.22 ± 0.20 ×10⁻⁵ (1/s); KL = 41.4 ± 7.28 ×10³ (cm). Effective diffusion area 1.77 cm². Sampling every 1 h for 8 h after 1-h acclimation.
Environmental condition: T50RH55 (50°C, 55% RH, AH 45.6 g/m³, skin surface temp 40°C). Log P = −6.01 ± 0.046; DL⁻² = 3.12 ± 0.43 ×10⁻⁵ (1/s); KL = 32.2 ± 7.48 ×10³ (cm).
Environmental condition: T52RH30 (52°C, 30% RH, AH 27.3 g/m³, skin surface temp 40°C). Log P = −6.02 ± 0.053; DL⁻² = 4.13 ± 0.67 ×10⁻⁵ (1/s); KL = 24.0 ± 7.20 ×10³ (cm).
Environmental condition: T27RH55 (27°C, 55% RH, AH 14.2 g/m³, skin surface temp 29°C). Log P = −6.15 ± 0.089; DL⁻² = 1.95 ± 0.38 ×10⁻⁵ (1/s); KL = 37.2 ± 7.57 ×10³ (cm). Reference/control condition.

notes: Table III (ENDPOINT_TABLE_HINT) reports skin permeation parameters for ibuprofen (IP) and ANP across four environmental conditions. Only ibuprofen rows are extracted here. The endpoint captured is Log P (log permeability coefficient), which is the primary comparative endpoint reported. DL⁻² (diffusivity parameter) and KL (partition parameter) are also present in Table III but are encoded in the notes field rather than as separate records because endpoint_kind does not have a matching enum for those parameter types. Vehicle composition is from Table I (page 3). The donor was a saturated solution of ibuprofen in the vehicle; exact mg/mL API concentration was not tabulated. Table III does not report cumulative amount (Q) or flux (Jss) directly — only derived parameters. ENDPOINT_TABLE_HINT Table 3 pages were truncated and Table 3 (full permeation data with Q values) was not captured in the provided pages; only Table III on page 6 was available. Recommended_next set to need_more_pages because Table 3 (cumulative permeation / Jss values) referenced in the hint may be on pages not included in SELECTED_LOCATORS.

title: Transcutol® P/Cremophor® EL/Ethyl Oleate-Formulated Microemulsion Loaded into Hyaluronic Acid-Based Hydrogel for Improved Transdermal Delivery and Biosafety of Ibuprofen.

formulations.evidence_snippet: In vitro transdermal permeation profiles (a) and transdermal fluxes (b) of ibuprofen from ibuprofen-loaded microemulsion (ME) and ibuprofen-loaded microemulsion-based hyaluronic acid hydrogel (ME/Gel). Compared with ME/Gel, **p < 0.01. (n = 3)
formulations.notes: Table I composition values truncated in PDF text. Endpoint values are from Fig. 3 (graphical) — cumulative permeation ~400 µg/cm² at 24 h for ME/Gel, read from figure description; exact numerical values not explicitly tabulated. Dose: 1 g preparation applied to donor cell.

formulations.evidence_snippet: In vitro transdermal permeation profiles (a) and transdermal fluxes (b) of ibuprofen from ME/Gel, Gel, Cream formulations. (n = 3)
formulations.notes: Table I composition values truncated in PDF text. Endpoint values are from Fig. 3 (graphical) — cumulative permeation lower than ME/Gel at 24 h; exact numerical values not explicitly tabulated. Dose: 1 g preparation.

components.remark: commercial comparator product; purchased from SK&F (Tianjin, China)

formulations.evidence_snippet: In vitro transdermal permeation profiles (a) and transdermal fluxes (b) of ibuprofen from ME/Gel, Gel, Cream formulations. (n = 3)
formulations.notes: Commercial cream comparator. Composition not provided in paper. Endpoint values from Fig. 3 (graphical); exact numerical values not explicitly tabulated. Dose: 1 g preparation.

notes: Table I (composition of ibuprofen-loaded formulations: ME/Gel, Gel) is referenced and partially described in the text but its numeric values are truncated in the provided PDF page text. The three formulations compared in vitro are ME/Gel (microemulsion-HA hydrogel), Gel (HA hydrogel only), and Cream (commercial comparator). In vitro permeation endpoint data (cumulative permeation µg/cm² vs. time up to 24 h and transdermal flux µg/cm²/h) are presented in Fig. 3 as graphs, not as explicit numerical tables, so endpoint_value fields are left null. No explicit endpoint summary table was found in the provided pages. Membrane: excised rat abdominal skin, Franz diffusion cell; dose: 1 g applied to donor cell. TABLE_TRUNCATED flag applies — full Table I numeric values were not recoverable.

title: Use of Silicone Membrane Permeation to Assess Thermodynamic Activities of Ionic Liquids and Their Component Cation and Anion.

components.basis: ~3× solubility; suspended in purified water at 37°C for 24 h

formulations.evidence_snippet: ibuprofen permeation rates from the suspended aqueous solution were 7.92 ± 0.39 and 2.35 ± 0.20 µmol/cm²/h, respectively (mean ± S.D., n = 3). The dQ/dtsol(Av = 1) was the membrane permeation rate of lidocaine or ibuprofen when their activity in water was 1.0
formulations.notes: Steady-state flux from ibuprofen-suspended aqueous solution; dQ/dtsol(Av=1) = 2.35 ± 0.20 µmol/cm²/h (mean±SD, n=3). Used as reference Av=1 for theoretical calculations (Eq. 4).

notes: Table 2 (page 2–3) provides molar fraction compositions of 11 IL preparations (#0–#10) based on lidocaine/ibuprofen mixtures. Each preparation row is extracted as a separate record. Ibuprofen is the anionic API throughout. Endpoint values (steady-state flux, fluxss) for individual preparations #1–9 are presented only graphically (Figs. 3, 4) and not in tabular form in the available pages; therefore endpoint_value is null for those records. One explicit in-text quantitative endpoint is reported for the ibuprofen-suspended aqueous solution reference (dQ/dtsol(Av=1) = 2.35 ± 0.20 µmol/cm²/h, n=3). Preparation #10 is pure lidocaine with zero ibuprofen. All permeation experiments used: silicone membrane 75 µm (LINTEC), PBS pH 7.4 receiver, 37°C, donor volume 3.0 mL, fluxss calculated from slope of cumulative permeation 1–8 h. TABLE_TRUNCATED flag is set; pages beyond page 5 not available so additional tables (if any) may be missing.

title: A new vesicle-loaded hydrogel system suitable for topical applications: preparation and characterization.

formulations.notes: Table 1 characterization data: Size 150±2 nm, p.i. 0.130±0.01, zeta-potential -44±2 mV, EE 0.4±0.1%. Release experiments carried out at 32°C on non-purified samples (vesicles not purified before embedding in gel).

formulations.formulation_label: NSV-IBU/gel (niosomes-gel system, IBU in NSVs embedded in LBG/xanthan hydrogel)
formulations.dosage_form: niosome-loaded hydrogel (LBG/xanthan 1:1 w/w, 0.5% w/v each polymer; NSVs Tween 20/cholesterol 1:1)

components.remark: pH 7.4, vesicle hydration and release medium

formulations.evidence_snippet: even after 48 h, the release was not complete and only about 60% of the loaded drug was capable to diffuse from the matrix (Figure 3a)
formulations.notes: Table 2 endpoint hinted. Explicit value from text: ~60% IBU released after 48 h from NSVs-gel system. Release experiment at 32°C in 200 mL HEPES buffer. Gel prepared at 60°C with LBG/xanthan 1:1 weight ratio; vesicle volume 2 mL added to 4 mL each polymer solution. Value read from Figure 3a narrative in text as no numerical Table 2 was fully extracted (truncated). Confidence reduced accordingly.

formulations.dosage_form: hydrogel (LBG/xanthan 1:1 w/w, 0.5% w/v each polymer; no NSVs)
formulations.evidence_snippet: there was only a slight difference between the two release profiles during the first 8 hours
formulations.notes: Table 2 endpoint hinted. Comparator gel formulation (no NSVs) for IBU release shown in Figure 3a. Numerical endpoint value not explicitly stated for this arm in text; only slight difference vs NSV-gel noted during first 8 h. Table 2 content not fully available due to PDF truncation.

notes: Table 2 was hinted as the endpoint table but was not rendered in the extracted PDF text (truncated before Table 2 could be parsed). Table 1 (vesicle characterization) was fully extracted and one IBU row is reported. IBU release endpoint values (~60% at 48 h from NSV-gel system) were taken from the narrative text of page 5 and Figure 3a description, not from a numeric table. Confidence is accordingly reduced for endpoint rows. All formulations use Tween 20/cholesterol 1:1 (15:15 mM) niosomes in LBG/xanthan (1:1 w/w, 0.5% w/v each) hydrogel; release conducted at 32°C in 200 mL HEPES buffer pH 7.4. No membrane used (beaker/bulk release setup, not Franz cell). recommended_next set to need_more_pages to retrieve Table 2 numeric data.
